title: Chapter 2: Machine Learning With Python Projects - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of ML project workflow, preprocessing, model selection, and evaluation

questions.question: What is the primary difference between supervised and unsupervised learning?

options.text: Supervised learning is faster than unsupervised learning
Supervised learning uses labeled data with known outputs for training, while unsupervised learning finds patterns in data without predefined labels
Supervised learning requires more data than unsupervised learning
Unsupervised learning always produces more accurate results

questions.explanation: In supervised learning, the model is trained on labeled data where the correct output is known (e.g., classification labels or regression targets). In unsupervised learning, the model finds hidden patterns or structure in unlabeled data (e.g., clustering or dimensionality reduction). The choice depends on whether labeled data is available and the task at hand.

questions.question: What is overfitting in machine learning?

options.text: When a model performs poorly on both training and test data
When a model learns the training data too well, including noise and outliers, and performs poorly on new unseen data
When a model is trained for too few epochs
When the training dataset is too large for the model

questions.explanation: Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too well — capturing noise, outliers, and random fluctuations rather than the underlying pattern. An overfit model has high accuracy on training data but poor performance on test/unseen data. Techniques to combat overfitting include regularization, dropout, early stopping, and using more training data.

questions.question: What is cross-validation and why is it used?

options.text: A method of training multiple models simultaneously on different GPUs
A technique that splits data into multiple folds, trains on different subsets, and averages the results to get a more reliable estimate of model performance
A way to validate that the data has been correctly formatted
A method of checking if the model's code compiles without errors

questions.explanation: Cross-validation (e.g., k-fold) divides the dataset into k equal folds. The model is trained k times, each time using a different fold as the validation set and the remaining k-1 folds for training. The results are averaged to produce a more robust performance estimate that reduces the variance caused by a single train/test split.

questions.question: Which evaluation metric is most appropriate for a binary classification problem with imbalanced classes?

options.text: Accuracy alone
Mean Squared Error
Precision, Recall, and F1-score
R-squared (R²)

questions.explanation: With imbalanced classes (e.g., 95% negative, 5% positive), accuracy is misleading — a model that always predicts the majority class achieves 95% accuracy. Precision (of predicted positives, how many are correct), Recall (of actual positives, how many were found), and F1-score (harmonic mean of precision and recall) give a much better picture. MSE and R² are regression metrics.

questions.question: What is the purpose of feature scaling (e.g., StandardScaler) in a machine learning pipeline?

options.text: To increase the number of features in the dataset
To transform features to a similar scale so that algorithms sensitive to feature magnitude (like SVM, KNN, gradient descent) perform correctly
To remove irrelevant features from the dataset
To convert categorical features to numerical values

questions.explanation: Feature scaling ensures that all features contribute proportionally to the model. Algorithms like SVM, KNN, and neural networks (which use gradient descent) are sensitive to feature magnitudes. Without scaling, a feature ranging 0-1000 would dominate over one ranging 0-1. StandardScaler transforms features to zero mean and unit variance.

questions.question: What is underfitting in machine learning?

options.text: When the model has too many parameters for the available data
When the model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data, resulting in poor performance on both training and test data
When the model achieves perfect accuracy on the test set
When the training set is larger than the test set

questions.explanation: Underfitting occurs when the model is too simple (high bias) to capture the true relationship in the data. An underfit model performs poorly on both training and test data because it hasn't learned enough from the training data. Solutions include using a more complex model, adding features, reducing regularization, or training longer.

questions.question: What does the train_test_split function from scikit-learn do?

options.text: It trains a model and then tests it automatically
It randomly splits arrays or matrices into training and testing subsets based on a specified proportion
It splits the dataset based on time, with earlier data for training and later data for testing
It removes duplicate samples and splits the rest into two groups

questions.explanation: train_test_split randomly divides the input data and labels into training and testing subsets. The test_size parameter controls the proportion (e.g., 0.2 for 20% test data). The random_state parameter ensures reproducibility. Stratification can be applied to maintain class proportions in both sets.

questions.question: What is a confusion matrix used for?

options.text: To visualize the architecture of a neural network
To display the counts of true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for a classification model
To identify which features are most important in the model
To measure the training time of different models

questions.explanation: A confusion matrix is a table that summarizes the performance of a classification model. For binary classification, it shows: True Positives (correctly predicted positive), True Negatives (correctly predicted negative), False Positives (incorrectly predicted positive — Type I error), and False Negatives (incorrectly predicted negative — Type II error). From these, precision, recall, and F1-score can be calculated.

questions.question: What is the bias-variance tradeoff?

options.text: The tradeoff between training speed and model accuracy
The tradeoff between a model's ability to minimize error on training data (bias) and its sensitivity to fluctuations in training data (variance)
The tradeoff between using more features and using less data
The tradeoff between model size on disk and prediction speed

questions.explanation: The bias-variance tradeoff is a fundamental concept in ML. High bias (underfitting) means the model is too simple and misses relevant patterns. High variance (overfitting) means the model is too sensitive to training data noise. The goal is to find the sweet spot that minimizes total error — a model complex enough to capture patterns but not so complex that it memorizes noise.

questions.question: Which preprocessing step is essential when dealing with categorical features like 'color' = ['red', 'blue', 'green'] in a machine learning model?

options.text: Removing all categorical columns from the dataset
Encoding them as numerical values using techniques like one-hot encoding or label encoding
Replacing them with random numbers
Sorting them alphabetically and using their position as the value

questions.explanation: Most ML algorithms require numerical input. Categorical features must be encoded: one-hot encoding creates binary columns for each category (avoiding false ordinal relationships), while label encoding assigns integers. One-hot encoding is preferred for nominal categories (no inherent order), while label/ordinal encoding can be suitable when categories have a natural order.
